maps.angle: Physics-informed pose refinement + foot-ground contact 2025-2026: differentiable-physics or learned contact models fixing foot skate without degrading fast-motion fidelity; sports-specific ground-reaction/pose refinement; measured foot-skate reductions vs classical foot-locking.

findings.claim: Multi-person physics-based pose estimation for combat sports (boxing) uses a dynamics-optimization stage (iLQR) with collision penalties on top of a kinematic HMR front-end; on their supplementary/interaction data, foot floating error dropped 16.4mm->8.1mm and foot sliding (horizontal velocity) error dropped 2.2mm->0.3mm going from kinematic-only to physics-refined output, with only marginal PCP/pose-accuracy loss even on Hi4D's high-contact-ratio fast-interaction clips.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly comparable axis to our foot-slide p95 8-23mm (tripod, classical foot-locking): this is a same-genre sport (fast athletic contact motion, not slow indoor) showing a physics-optimization pass (iLQR + collision terms) can push foot-sliding to sub-mm with only marginal accuracy cost during fast movement -- i.e. evidence that a differentiable-physics refinement stage COULD beat our classical stance-detection/foot-pin approach without the fast-swing degradation we saw from Fast-SAM-3D-Body. Not directly pluggable (multi-person combat-specific iLQR pipeline, not a drop-in library) but validates the approach class is worth a build-vs-buy look once MHR decode fidelity is fixed -- physics refinement is downstream of decode and would inherit its 262mm/53.5mm defect, so this is a wave-N+ item, not now.

findings.claim: OnlineHMR (arXiv 2603.17355, March 2026) is a causal/streaming world-grounded human mesh recovery method: two-branch architecture with a causal KV-cache for streaming inference plus a 'human-centric incremental SLAM' module for world-grounded alignment, explicitly targeting AR/VR and interactive perception-action loops where prior HMR (WHAM, TRAM, GVHMR-class) needed future frames or global optimization. Claims performance comparable to chunk-based offline approaches on EMDB and on 'highly dynamic custom videos,' but the abstract does not surface numeric foot-skate/jitter/world-grounding-error figures (full-text PDF was not text-extractable via fetch).
findings.relevance_to_us: A newer name than our known list (GVHMR/WHAM/TRAM) doing exactly our world-grounding problem (per-frame/online, causal, needs to ground to real-world scale/ground plane) via incremental SLAM rather than a decode-time scale correction -- worth a deeper read once someone has bandwidth, since our fresh defect is scale-application-in-decode being suspect; an external causal-SLAM grounding approach is a structurally different fix path than 'debug the decode.' Not load-bearing yet because no hard numbers were retrievable and it's unclear it addresses mesh-skeleton divergence (our actual defect) vs. camera-to-world grounding.

findings.claim: Physics-Informed Ground Reaction Dynamics from Human Motion Capture (arXiv 2507.01340, July 2025) estimates vertical ground reaction force (vGRF) and root trajectory (vRPE) using a PD-controller-derived physics term as an added training objective (root-joint-offset-derived reactive force), without needing force plates at inference; reports vRPE (x10^-3 m) of 14.69±13.37 vs an 38.43±22.56 baseline, and ~18% average vGRF improvement over the GroundLink baseline.
findings.relevance_to_us: Different track from ours (force/vGRF regression trained against force-plate-labeled datasets, not foot-skate-in-mm on a mesh), and no comparison to classical foot-locking/IK is given. Useful mainly as a data point that PD-based physics pseudo-labels can substitute for real force plates -- a training-time trick, not an inference-time fix -- so low relevance to our immediate foot-slide/decode-fidelity blockers.

findings.claim: Ground Reaction Inertial Poser (GRIP, arXiv 2603.16233) reconstructs physically-plausible motion from 4 IMUs + insole pressure sensors via a KinematicsNet + DynamicsNet (residual-driven humanoid simulator control) pipeline.
findings.relevance_to_us: Dead end for us: requires worn IMU+pressure-insole hardware, not video-only monocular capture, which is a hard product-scope mismatch (we have no player-worn sensors and no plan to add them).

dead_ends: GRIP / Ground Reaction Inertial Poser and similar IMU+pressure-insole physics posers: require worn sensors, out of scope for a monocular-video pickleball pipeline.

Physics-informed Ground Reaction Dynamics (2507.01340) is a force/vGRF-estimation paper trained against force-plate datasets -- not a mesh foot-skate-in-mm benchmark and gives no classical-foot-locking baseline comparison, so it doesn't answer the 'does physics beat our foot-pin numbers' question directly.

Could not retrieve full-text numeric tables from OnlineHMR's PDF (binary/compressed) or find a working link for 'Gravity-guided Contact Dynamics Estimation' (2606.08133) -- search returned no matching result for that arXiv ID, likely a bad/hallucinated-looking ID from the first search pass; do not cite it further without re-verifying the ID.

maps.angle: Monocular multi-person 3D human mesh recovery in WORLD coordinates (late 2025-2026): what beats WHAM/TRAM/GVHMR-class now, metric world-frame errors (W-MPJPE, foot skate), and anything sports/fast-motion-specific or SAM-3D-Body-specific that bears on our decode-fidelity defect and unwired latent smoothing.

findings.claim: 'World-Coordinate Human Motion Retargeting via SAM 3D Body' builds directly on top of our exact model (SAM 3D Body / MHR) as a frozen backbone, adding: (1) temporally-consistent bone lengths by LOCKING identity/skeleton params across a window, (2) sliding-window optimization performed IN THE MOMENTUM HUMAN RIG LATENT SPACE (not on the decoded mesh) to smooth predictions, and (3) a differentiable soft foot-ground contact model + contact-aware global optimization to recover physically plausible root trajectories, feeding a Unitree G1 IK retargeter. No W-MPJPE/foot-skate-mm numbers surfaced in the abstract.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the closest published precedent for our exact two open problems: (a) our latent temporal smoothing is currently UNWIRED pending decode fidelity <=1mm — this paper claims smoothing works by locking skeleton/identity params and optimizing in MHR latent space, which is a candidate design for our smoother once decode is trusted, and may also suggest that locking scale/identity per-track could be a workaround for the decode-internal scale defect itself (bypass rather than fix). (b) our grounding_refine stage is KILLED and blocked on decode fidelity — their soft foot-contact + contact-aware global optimization is a published alternative grounding approach worth benchmarking once we un-kill.

findings.claim: 'Investigating Anthropometric Fidelity in SAM 3D Body' (independent audit of SAM 3D Body/MHR) finds the model exhibits a 'regression to the mean' effect on individual body shape/scale, attributed to three architectural causes: the low-dimensional MHR parametric representation itself, semantic-invariant DINOv3 conditioning, and annotation-based alignment mechanisms used in training — i.e., scale/shape fidelity loss is described as baked into the representation and alignment pipeline, not a bug, though no worst-joint-mm numbers are given in the abstract.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly on-topic for our fresh decode-fidelity defect (262mm worst-joint, 53.5mm p95 mesh-skeleton divergence, decode-internal, scale-application suspect). This is a third-party paper confirming SAM-3D-Body/MHR has known, architecturally-rooted scale/shape fidelity limits (not just an implementation bug in our harness) — raises the possibility our defect is partly an upstream model property rather than purely our decode-plumbing bug, which should temper how much further internal debugging we sink into 'find the exact scale bug' vs. treating some residual error as a model ceiling to work around (e.g. via constraints/locking as in the retargeting paper above).

findings.claim: 'SAM-Body4D: Training-Free 4D Human Body Mesh Recovery from Videos' adds temporal consistency to SAM 3D Body without any retraining, by using an identity-consistent 'masklet' promptable video segmentation model plus an occlusion-aware refinement module to condition SAM 3D Body's per-frame prompts, rather than smoothing the output mesh/latent post-hoc. No jitter/foot-skate/world-coordinate numeric results in the abstract.
findings.relevance_to_us: Alternative architecture for temporal stability: smooth via better/consistent PROMPTS (segmentation masklets) into SAM-3D-Body rather than smoothing the decoded output or latent trajectory after the fact. Since our post-chain currently does person-track -> court placement -> stance -> smoothing on the decoded/skeleton side, this suggests a different lever (upstream masklet conditioning) that could reduce jitter before decode fidelity is even fixed — worth a cheap eval given our smoothing stage is currently blocked.

findings.claim: 'Fast SAM 3D Body: Accelerating SAM 3D Body for Real-Time Full-Body Human Mesh Recovery' exists as a distinct, newer paper from the one we already benched NOT-ADOPT — confirm whether this is the same lineage.
findings.relevance_to_us: Flag only — likely the same Fast-SAM-3D-Body already benched NOT-ADOPT in our notes (accuracy regression at fast swings, slower wall per-clip), but the date (March 2026) is newer than our bench; worth a 30-second check that our NOT-ADOPT ruling covered this exact paper/checkpoint and not an older variant, since 'fast swings' regression is precisely our sport's failure mode.

findings.claim: OnlineHMR (CVPR 2026 poster) is a new streaming/causal world-coordinate HMR framework using a causal key-value cache for real-time AR/VR use, claiming performance 'comparable to existing chunk-based approaches on the standard EMDB benchmark' — but it is a generic single-camera-track method (not SAM-3D-Body-based, not sports-specific), and no W-MPJPE/foot-skate numbers were retrievable from the abstract.
findings.relevance_to_us: Lower priority: it's a WHAM/GVHMR-class competitor and genuinely newer/different from our known names, but it targets low-latency streaming (AR/VR/telepresence) not accuracy, and doesn't address our body/mesh source (SAM-3D-Body) or our specific decode-fidelity defect. Only relevant if we ever reconsider our BODY backbone entirely, which is not indicated.

findings.claim: CalTennis (large multi-view tennis video dataset/benchmark, 11M+ frames, 40 players) and TT4D (table-tennis 4D reconstruction incl. ball spin) are new 2026 sports-specific 3D-pose benchmarks/datasets for racket sports, but neither targets pickleball nor reports foot-skate/W-MPJPE comparable to our harness; golf-swing work found still relies on WHAM (older backbone), not SAM-3D-Body-class methods.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms no pickleball-specific or SAM-3D-Body-based sports benchmark exists yet — our own harness remains the most relevant sports-fast-motion evaluation for this backbone. Low priority for roadmap changes now, but CalTennis's multi-view ground truth could eventually be a useful external validation set for our BODY module's fast-swing accuracy if licensing permits.

dead_ends: Direct search for numeric W-MPJPE / foot-skate-mm tables in OnlineHMR, SAM-Body4D, and the SAM-3D-Body retargeting paper abstracts came up empty via WebFetch summarization of the abstract pages — would need to pull the actual PDFs/tables (not just arXiv abstract page) to get numbers; not done here given time budget.
No pickleball-specific monocular mesh recovery paper found anywhere in 2025-2026 literature; racket-sport work (tennis/table-tennis/golf) is the closest proxy and none of it uses SAM-3D-Body/MHR yet.

Could not confirm from abstracts alone whether Fast-SAM-3D-Body (2603.15603) is the identical paper/checkpoint we already benched NOT-ADOPT, or a revision — needs a quick full-text diff before treating as 'new' finding.

maps.angle: Meta MHR (Momentum Human Rig) + SAM-3D-Body decode internals: latent pose-code → world mesh round-trip, scale/translation/coordinate conventions, known pitfalls reconstructing world joints from persisted latents, and any latent-space temporal smoothing prior art. Searched arXiv (2511.15586 MHR, 2512.21573 world-coord retargeting, 2602.15989 SAM-3D-Body) and GitHub issues on facebookresearch/sam-3d-body and facebookresearch/MHR (2025-11 through 2026-06).

findings.claim: pred_vertices from run_inference.py does NOT include global translation — translation lives separately in pred_cam_t. Correct world/scene placement requires pred_vertices + pred_cam_t[None,...]; using pred_vertices alone (treating it as already-placed) silently gives a body sitting near the camera origin with translation stripped out.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is a strong candidate root cause for our 'decode-internal, none-vs-field-scale changed nothing' defect: if the harness (or the underlying decode call) is not correctly adding pred_cam_t to pred_vertices before world placement — or is adding it twice, or applying it to the mesh but not the skeleton (or vice versa) — you get exactly the mesh-vs-skeleton divergence (53.5mm p95) we're seeing, independent of any scale field. Load-bearing: re-audit the exact line where pred_cam_t is combined with pred_vertices vs. pred_joint_coords in our decode wrapper before concluding the bug is 'inside' MHR's black box.

findings.claim: A Meta contributor (JinlongYANG) confirms the canonical way to reconstruct an MHR mesh from persisted SAM-3D-Body outputs requires exactly 4 fields — mhr_model_params (model_parameters), shape_params (identity_coeffs), expr_params (face_expr_coeffs), and pred_vertices (as the global-translation-bearing field) — and points to a maintained reference implementation: MHR/tools/mhr_smpl_conversion/conversion.py, function Conversion.convert_sam3d_output_to_smpl() (stop at line ~512 for MHR-only, i.e. skip the SMPL conversion part).
findings.relevance_to_us: This is a ground-truth, maintainer-endorsed reference decode path we should diff our own decode-and-place code against line-by-line — it is the single best artifact to resolve the 'scale application inside decode is suspect' open question. If our custom decode diverges from this reference (e.g. skips a correctives step, applies identity_coeffs at wrong LOD, or mishandles expr_params defaults), that is very likely the 262mm-worst-joint bug.

findings.claim: Multiple independent users (issues #60, #92, #65, #34) report the same class of confusion/instability: body scale/size appears to shift frame-to-frame on video (no persistent identity/shape lock across frames), global_trans in the MHR head forward is literally hardcoded to torch.zeros_like(...) (i.e. the network's own 'global rotation' output has a zero placeholder for translation baked into mhr_head.py), and the online Playground visualization uses a separate 'fake_pred_cam_t' heuristic (median of vertex bbox extremes) rather than the model's real translation output for multi-person scenes.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms per-frame scale/identity is NOT temporally locked by the model itself — SAM-3D-Body treats every frame as an independent single-image reconstruction with no persistent shape/scale prior. This matches and explains why our post-chain smoothing can't fix decode-internal error (it's per-frame independent, not a temporal artifact) and reinforces that any fix belongs either in a custom shape-locking wrapper (fit identity_coeffs once, hold fixed, only let pose vary) or upstream before smoothing is unwired.

findings.claim: 'World-Coordinate Human Motion Retargeting via SAM 3D Body' (arXiv 2512.21573, Dec 2025) is a brand-new paper explicitly building a world-coordinate reconstruction + temporal smoothing pipeline on top of SAM-3D-Body for motion retargeting to humanoid robots — i.e., someone else has already built approximately our post-chain (world placement + temporal smoothing on SAM-3D-Body output) for a different downstream task.
findings.relevance_to_us: Newer than our known-names list and directly on-angle (world-coord + temporal smoothing on top of SAM-3D-Body). Full-text extraction was blocked (PDF returned compressed/unparsed by the fetch tool), so equations/exact scale-handling weren't retrievable this pass — worth a follow-up WebFetch attempt on the HTML version (arxiv.org/html/2512.21573) or a dedicated deep-read, since it may contain a working recipe for exactly the world-placement + smoothing problem we have stalled on pending decode fidelity.

findings.claim: Issue #47 'Are predictions (approximately) metrically accurate?' exists as an open, apparently unanswered question on the repo (no visible maintainer response in the fetched thread) about whether SAM-3D-Body's metric-scale claims hold up in practice.
findings.relevance_to_us: Corroborates (from an independent line of community inquiry) that metric-scale trustworthiness of SAM-3D-Body/MHR output is an open, contested question rather than a solved/documented guarantee — supports keeping VERIFIED=0 and not assuming metric fidelity even after a decode fix ships.

dead_ends: arXiv 2512.21573 full text could not be extracted via WebFetch (PDF came back as compressed/binary metadata only, not readable prose) — only title/keywords/author names were recoverable this pass; a second attempt via the HTML mirror or a dedicated fetch-and-summarize pass is needed before treating it as fully mined.

Direct GitHub search for MHR-repo issues mentioning 'latent' or 'decode' returned no new items beyond #39/#27 (both already covered) — the MHR repo itself has very few issues total (~3), so most real discussion of decode/scale problems lives in the downstream sam-3d-body repo, not MHR.
Issue #92 (World Coordinate System Back-Calculation for Static Camera Positions) is open with no maintainer resolution yet — useful as corroboration but not as a fix recipe.

No GitHub issues or papers found describing anyone doing genuine latent-pose-code-space temporal smoothing (i.e. smoothing model_parameters/identity_coeffs directly pre-decode) — all found prior art (including 2512.21573, per keywords) appears to smooth in decoded/world joint space, not latent code space, so our planned latent-smoothing approach appears to be ahead of any documented public prior art rather than duplicating one.

maps.angle: Web playback of per-frame human mesh sequences 2025-2026: compression codecs, client-side temporal interpolation, streaming formats, and what volumetric-video products actually use

findings.claim: MPEG-I Part 29 'V-DMC' (Video-based Dynamic Mesh Coding) reached FDIS (Final Draft International Standard) in July 2025, with full IS publication expected March 2026. It's purpose-built for dynamic triangular meshes of volumetric humans/avatars: encodes a base mesh + per-frame displacement fields, packs displacements into 2D video frames, and reuses existing hardware video codecs (HEVC/VVC) for the heavy lifting. Comparative testing shows it beats Draco on bitrate in both lossless and lossy modes.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is a NEWER, purpose-built successor to our likely default (Draco) specifically for the 'per-frame human mesh sequence' problem we have — base-mesh+displacement is close in spirit to what a MHR-decoded mesh sequence needs (shared topology per person, per-frame deformation). No browser/JS decoder exists yet per the standard docs (only Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 mobile real-time decode is referenced) — so it's not adoptable today, but worth a calendar flag for whenever reference decoders land, since it could directly attack our byte-budget mesh index problem (100-400MiB/clip).

findings.claim: glTF's two competing mesh-compression extensions are diverging in practice: KHR_draco_mesh_compression (Edgebreaker-based, ~95% size reduction on large geometry) is well-supported but is explicitly INCOMPATIBLE with morph-target animation (Draco does not compress morph targets) and has slower decode. As of 2025, MeshOpt (EXT_meshopt_compression + KHR_mesh_quantization, 8/16-bit quantized vertices, ~60% per-vertex memory savings) is gaining adoption in performance-critical/animated web-3D apps specifically because it decodes faster and works with animated/morphed geometry.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly actionable and cheap: if/when we serialize MHR meshes to glTF/GLB for browser playback, Draco is the wrong choice for our per-frame animated mesh (same identical-topology-different-vertex-position problem morph targets have) — MeshOpt+KHR_mesh_quantization is the format the field is converging on for exactly this animated-mesh case, and it's an off-the-shelf swap, not a research bet.

findings.claim: DeltaStream (MobiSys 2025, published Oct 2025) proposes 2D-inferred delta encoding for LIVE volumetric video streaming: instead of encoding full 3D deltas, it infers frame-to-frame geometry deltas from 2D video motion vectors/optical flow, cutting encode cost and bandwidth for real-time volumetric capture-to-stream pipelines.
findings.relevance_to_us: Conceptually adjacent to our decode-fidelity defect discussion (latent temporal smoothing unwired until decode <=1mm) — a 2D-video-motion-inferred delta approach is a genuinely different lever than the latent-space/vertex-space smoothing we've been pursuing, but this is a live-capture streaming optimization, not a post-hoc offline-inference mesh problem like ours, so it's informative rather than directly load-bearing for the playback/compression angle specifically.

findings.claim: DNE (Digital Nation Entertainment) + Gracia shipped what's billed as the first real-time-streamed 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) performance capture playable in any web browser with no app/download, in April 2026 — explicitly moving from mesh-based volumetric capture to radiance-field (Gaussian splat) representation to preserve soft edges/subsurface detail that meshes flatten. Separately, 'Spark 2.0' (Three.js/WebGL2-based) added an LoD system in 2026 for streaming large 3DGS scenes to any browser/device including mobile and VR.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the single most roadmap-relevant finding: the commercial volumetric-video industry is NOT converging on 'better mesh compression' for human capture at all — it's abandoning meshes for Gaussian splats specifically because mesh playback loses soft-tissue/motion detail (the exact class of fidelity loss we're fighting with our 262mm decode round-trip defect). If web-browser 4DGS-for-humans keeps maturing, it's a candidate alternative rendering target that sidesteps our whole mesh-decode-fidelity problem rather than requiring us to fix it — worth flagging as a possible pivot point once decode-fidelity work stalls, not an immediate action.

dead_ends: V^3 (arxiv 2409.13648, 'Viewing Volumetric Videos on Mobiles via Streamable 2D Dynamic Gaussians') and 4D-MoDe (arxiv 2509.17506) are both Gaussian-splat streaming papers, not mesh-specific — same direction as the DNE/Gracia finding above, redundant once that's captured.

DeformStream (arxiv 2409.16615, deformation-based adaptive volumetric streaming) and VV-DASH (ACM MMSys 2025) are DASH/adaptive-bitrate streaming-infrastructure papers (chunking/ABR logic), not compression codecs — orthogonal to our byte-budget-index problem which is about per-frame payload size, not adaptive network delivery; not pursued further.

Could not get readable content from the DeltaStream PDF via WebFetch (binary/unparseable) — only got the abstract-level takeaway from the search snippet, so bandwidth/fps numbers for DeltaStream are NOT verified, only the high-level approach.

No browser-side / JS reference decoder for MPEG V-DMC was found — standard docs only mention Snapdragon mobile native decode, so 'client-side temporal mesh interpolation in-browser for V-DMC' is unconfirmed/likely nonexistent as of Jul 2026; did not chase this further since it's a dead end until IS ships.

maps.angle: Speeding up ViT-class per-frame 3D body inference pipelines (2025-2026): compiler/quantization gains on H100, batching strategies, distillation that preserved accuracy, and frame-selective/track-then-densify temporal propagation — with concrete accuracy deltas, to find x-factors beyond our NOT-ADOPT Fast-SAM-3D-Body result.

findings.claim: SAM-Body4D (built directly on Meta SAM-3D-Body + SAM 3): training-free track-then-densify pipeline — SAM 3 generates identity-consistent 'masklets' across frames, an Occlusion-Aware Masklet Refiner (Diffusion-VAS) fills hidden body regions, then Mask-Guided HMR uses the refined masks as spatial prompts back into SAM 3D Body per-frame. On A100-SXM4-80GB, batched parallel inference (batch=32) over 90 frames/5 persons runs ~2x faster than sequential per-frame inference; qualitatively it prevents 'missing meshes' during occlusion that vanilla per-frame SAM-3D-Body produces. No FPS/absolute-latency numbers or MPJPE/PVE deltas reported — only qualitative continuity claims.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the closest available comparator to our own stack: same base model (SAM-3D-Body/MHR), same track-then-densify shape we're asking about, and it explicitly targets the occlusion gaps that map to our human_review-tier unmeshed frames (742/1151 on one clip). Its 2x batching win is a much smaller, much safer claim than Fast-SAM-3D-Body's regression-prone rewrite — worth a cheap internal bench (batch body+hand crops together) before touching decode. Since it operates on masks/prompts rather than re-deriving geometry, it should not interact with our decode-fidelity defect, so it's a candidate to de-risk in parallel with the decode fix rather than wait on it.

findings.claim: TemPoFast3D ('Link to the Past: Temporal Propagation for Fast 3D Human Reconstruction from Monocular Video') keeps a canonical-space human representation refined across frames via SMPL deformation-matrix coordinate mapping; does full coarse-to-fine reconstruction for only the first n=5 frames to seed geometric priors, then runs a cheaper propagation-only inference mode for the rest. Reports 3.77x speedup (3.3 -> 12.3 FPS max, via TorchScript) while holding PSNR >=30.96dB, SSIM >=0.98, LPIPS <=0.011; evaluated with GTA/SIFU pixel-aligned backbones on THuman2.0/CAPE/NeuMan, using Chamfer/P2S rather than MPJPE/PVE.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly matches our 'frame-selective inference w/ temporal propagation' angle and is conceptually the un-kill path for our latent temporal smoothing (currently unwired pending decode fidelity <=1mm): seed frames get full inference, remaining frames get cheap canonical-space propagation. Different backbone family (SIFU/GTA clothed-recon, not MHR/SMPL-X ViT), so it's a pattern to adapt rather than a drop-in — but it's evidence that seed-then-propagate can hit >3x speedup without an accuracy regression, which is the opposite of what our Fast-SAM-3D-Body bench found. Not load-bearing for immediate roadmap change since decode-fidelity blocks any temporal-smoothing work regardless, but should inform the design once decode is fixed.

findings.claim: OnlineHMR (CVPR 2026 poster), built on the TRAM architecture, achieves fully causal/online HMR via causal key-value caching + sliding-window learning for streaming inference, plus incremental SLAM for world-coordinate alignment with trajectory correction. Claims accuracy 'comparable to existing chunk-based [offline] approaches' on EMDB and custom dynamic videos, satisfying causality, efficiency, faithfulness, and temporal consistency simultaneously. No quantitative latency or MPJPE numbers surfaced in the abstract/poster page.
findings.relevance_to_us: Newer than our known WHAM/TRAM/GVHMR baselines and directly targets the causal/streaming world-grounding problem our post-chain does classically (person track -> court placement -> world grounding). Its incremental-SLAM + trajectory-correction approach is a plausible alternative/supplement to our killed grounding_refine stage, but un-kill there is explicitly blocked on decode fidelity, and this finding lacks hard numbers to act on yet — flag for a deeper read once decode is fixed, not load-bearing now.

findings.claim: SAM3DBody-cpp: a from-scratch, dependency-free C++ ONNX+ggml runtime reimplementation of SAM-3D-Body for real-time multi-person full-body reconstruction (70-joint skeleton with hands, multi-person BVH output), positioned as a production/edge-deployment path rather than a research accuracy improvement.
findings.source_date: 2026 (repo active, exact commit date not confirmed)
findings.relevance_to_us: Different axis from compute optimization research: this is a pure-inference-engine reimplementation (no PyTorch/Python), which could sidestep our torch.compile/TensorRT tuning question entirely for the decode/inference leg if it proves numerically equivalent — but given our fresh decode-fidelity defect is suspected inside scale/hand_pose application during decode, adopting a third-party reimplementation before our own root-cause is understood would risk masking or duplicating the bug. Treat as a future engineering option, not urgent; unverified accuracy parity, so not load-bearing.

findings.claim: General H100 compiler landscape update: torch.compile + CUDA graphs consistently outperformed TensorRT across most tested models in 2026 benchmarks (marginal gap for larger models), while a separate 2026 benchmark reports Torch-TensorRT reaching up to 6x speedup over eager PyTorch on GPUs generally, and TensorRT SDXL-specific gains of ~40% on H100. NVIDIA's FP8 Transformer Engine on H100 is documented (2026) at ~2x throughput / 2x memory reduction vs FP16/BF16 for transformer workloads generally; no SAM-3D-Body/MHR-specific FP8 or torch.compile benchmark was found anywhere in this pass.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms the generic compiler/quantization levers (torch.compile, FP8 Transformer Engine) are mature and de-risked on H100 for ViT-shaped models in general, so if a future lane wants to shave the steady-state 15.2ms/person-frame further without touching the decode-fidelity bug, torch.compile is the safer first lever (broadly applicable, no accuracy claims found either way) versus TensorRT (mixed results, needs model-specific validation) or FP8 (no HMR-specific accuracy-preservation evidence surfaced — would need our own bench before trusting it near a barely-verified decode path). Not load-bearing: this is generic infra info, not a roadmap-changing finding, and directly touching the decode path with quantization right now is inadvisable while decode-fidelity is still being root-caused.

dead_ends: FP8 quantization H100 + ViT + human pose/mesh 2026: no SAM-3D-Body/MHR-specific or ViT-HMR-specific FP8 accuracy-preservation study found; only generic FP8-on-H100 infra claims (2x throughput/memory) and unrelated FP8-BERT/LLM papers surfaced.

ViTKD / ViT-Linearizer / VITA (DAC 2024) distillation papers surfaced but are either generic ViT distillation (not HMR-specific) or predate our 2025-2026 window (VITA is 2024) or lack reported accuracy deltas specific to MHR/SMPL-X body models — did not pursue further.

Direct search for 'SAM 3D Body batching throughput H100 2026' returned no model-specific H100 batching numbers; only Fast-SAM-3D-Body (already known/NOT-ADOPT), generic SAM3/SAM3.1 object-multiplexing throughput (8-12fps SAM3 video, 16->32fps multiplexed tracking) which is a different model (segmentation, not body mesh) and not directly transferable.

OnlineHMR CVPR 2026 poster page had no linked full paper/PDF surfaced in this pass -- latency and MPJPE numbers unconfirmed; would need direct arXiv fetch to get quantitative deltas before treating as load-bearing.

maps.angle: Diff our MHR decode/world-placement harness against two primary sources: (1) the maintainer-endorsed reference conversion.py::convert_sam3d_output_to_smpl in facebookresearch/MHR, and (2) facebookresearch/sam-3d-body GitHub issues #34 (spine-joint gap) and #82/#92/#65 (pred_cam_t / coordinate-system threads), to find which mechanism actually explains our 53.5mm p95 / 262mm worst-joint mesh-skeleton divergence.

findings.claim: The reference conversion.py (convert_sam3d_output_to_smpl, MHR repo, lines 447-516) operates ONLY on pred_vertices (mesh) + pred_cam_t — it never reads or touches pred_joint_coords or pred_keypoints_3d anywhere in the file (grep confirmed zero hits). So this 'maintainer-endorsed reference' has no blessed math at all for placing the 127-joint MHR skeleton (pred_joint_coords) in camera/world space — it only tells you how to place the mesh.
findings.relevance_to_us: Reframes the original scout question: there is no line-by-line joint-placement reference to diff against. If our harness derives world-space skeleton by applying vertex-style scale/cam_t math to pred_joint_coords, that's an unsupported extrapolation, not a verified-vs-reference comparison — worth flagging as a methodology gap in our own harness rather than assuming conversion.py validates or invalidates our joint math.

findings.claim: conversion.py explicitly scales BOTH pred_vertices and pred_cam_t by 100.0 before summing: 'mhr_vertices = 100.0*pred_vertices + 100.0*pred_cam_t[:,None,:]' with the comment 'SAM3D outputs the vertices in meters, so we need to convert them to centimeters before passing them to the conversion function' (MHR mesh space is centimeters). A second code path (mhr_model_params branch, line 509-512) additionally flips vertex Y/Z axes (`mhr_vertices[...,[1,2]] *= -1  # Camera system difference in SAM3D-Body`) BEFORE adding the same 100x-scaled cam_t — i.e. the two reference paths for reaching mesh-in-camera-space are not naively equivalent (one needs an axis flip, one doesn't), which is easy to get backwards.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly actionable: our harness's 'scale application inside the decode is suspect since none-vs-field changed nothing' finding should be re-tested specifically against 'is pred_cam_t scaled by 100x (m->cm) at exactly the same point/units as pred_vertices, and is the axis-flip applied on whichever code path we take.' A one-sided scale slip (e.g. vertices scaled by 100 but cam_t left in meters, or vice versa) produces an additive world-space offset on the order of tens of cm — consistent in kind with (though larger than) our 262mm worst-joint gap, and worth a literal side-by-side unit check before looking elsewhere.

findings.claim: facebookresearch/sam-3d-body issue #34 (opened 2025-11-20, closed out with maintainer 'zaino' reply 2025-11-23/25) confirms: pred_keypoints_3d is a SEPARATE, smaller joint set that structurally lacks central spine joints (only perimeter joints — shoulders/hips/limbs). The Playground's full skeleton (with spine) comes from `pred_joint_coords`, which is the MHR-native 126-bone armature PLUS one prepended 'body_world' joint = 127 total; the demo only renders the subset with names not ending in '_proc'. This is an independent, confirmed divergence mechanism: using pred_keypoints_3d instead of pred_joint_coords will structurally omit/misrepresent spine joints regardless of any cam_t/scale fix.
findings.relevance_to_us: High-priority check: confirm which field our decode harness reads for the 'skeleton' side of the mesh-skeleton comparison. If it's pred_keypoints_3d (or an internal SMPL-style joint regressor output) rather than pred_joint_coords, the spine-region divergence (plausibly the 'worst joint' in our 262mm figure, since spine/torso is exactly where this gap manifests) may be a joint-set/definition mismatch, not a decode math bug — independent of and possibly compounding the cam_t/scale issue above.

findings.claim: A separate, more recent maintainer comment (issue #82, 2026-01-05, user JinlongYANG i.e. a Meta MHR/SAM3D contributor) explicitly restates the cam_t contract for multi-person scenes: 'the global translation of those pred_vertices are not included in pred_vertices itself but in pred_cam_t ... if you have multiple people reconstructed in a single image, the camera should sit at the origin and the people are pred_vertices + pred_cam_t[None, ...]' and points to this same conversion.py line as the canonical reference.
findings.relevance_to_us: Corroborates (does not newly overturn) the wave-5/6 note that 'pred_cam_t addition' is the blessed placement mechanism; combined with the 100x scale finding above this is the single most concrete, testable diff point for our harness's world-placement code.

findings.claim: Issue #92 (opened 2025-12-13, unanswered as of fetch) asks the natural follow-on question we'd eventually hit: given only pred_cam_t (translation) per-frame, is there any model output for camera POSE/orientation, and can pred_cam_t alone back-calculate absolute body position for a fixed real-world camera? No maintainer answer exists yet.

maps.angle: Independent ground-truth validation of SAM-3D-Body/MHR decode metric fidelity (external to our pipeline)

findings.claim: GitHub issue #47 on facebookresearch/sam-3d-body, opened by user nlml on 2025-11-21, asks the exact open question: given a correct FOV estimate (or the true FOV fed in), would the predicted mesh have 'approximately correct metric dimensions' and would it 'still line up with the subject in world space' from different camera angles. As of the check, the issue is open with no maintainer response and no discussion thread — confirming this is a genuinely unanswered community question, not something we missed in existing docs.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly confirms the gap statement: nobody outside Meta has published or even proposed a methodology for independently ground-truthing SAM-3D-Body/MHR's raw metric decode. This means our 262mm/53.5mm defect cannot be cross-checked against a community-vetted external validation protocol — we are on our own to build one.

findings.claim: A closely related, already-answered defect exists in the sibling repo facebookresearch/sam-3d-objects, issue #57 (title: 'How to preserve real-world scale when using SAM3D with a real depth pointmap?'): a real depth pointmap of a 5cm cube round-tripped through SAM3D decoding came out ~15cm (3x scale error), i.e. the decoder does not preserve metric scale even when accurate depth is supplied as conditioning.
findings.source_date: 2025 (exact date not resolved in fetch, repo issue)
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the closest thing to an external, quantified 'known-object round-trip' ground-truth check found anywhere in the SAM-3D family, and it shows the identical failure signature we're seeing: decode-stage scale distortion that persists regardless of what scale/depth information is fed in ('none-vs-field changed nothing' in our own harness maps directly onto 'depth pointmap changed nothing' here). This is evidence our decode-internal hypothesis is plausible and consistent with a known class of bug in Meta's SAM-3D decoder family, not just our harness.

findings.claim: GitHub issue #69 on facebookresearch/sam-3d-body ('Children are adults, but very far'), opened by mfortini on 2025-11-28, documents that children/small-scale subjects get reconstructed as normal-sized adults placed at exaggerated distance — a systematic scale/depth confound baked into the decoder's body-proportion-based depth estimation (the focal length is estimated from image diagonal and baked into decoder conditioning, so the network associates apparent body size with metric depth, the classic SMPL/HMR monocular size-prior approach).
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms the decoder's metric-scale mechanism is a learned body-proportion-to-depth prior, not a geometric/calibration computation — meaning it has no independent metric anchor and is exactly the kind of component checkerboard/ArUco/known-dimension rigs would be needed to audit, since the model has never been validated against a physically-measured rig, only against benchmark datasets with their own annotation pipelines (keypoints + differentiable optimization, not physical ground truth).

findings.claim: The standard external validation methodology used in the wider human-mesh-recovery field is NOT checkerboard/ArUco rigs but marker-based motion capture comparison: OpenCap (markerless smartphone mocap) was validated against Vicon marker-based mocap on ACL-reconstruction patients performing jump-landing/hop tasks, finding RMSE of 4-10 degrees in joint angles (frontal hip <6°, sagittal knee 4-10°, other joints 4.04-7.66° RMSE) — this is a joint-angle comparison, not an absolute-position/scale comparison.
findings.relevance_to_us: This is the field-standard external validation pattern (marker-based mocap as ground truth) already flagged in our own memory (Deep review 2026-07-07 cites OpenCap as the recommended BODY validation route). But note it validates joint ANGLES/kinematics, not absolute metric scale/position — it would not by itself catch a 262mm world-space translation/scale error the way a known-dimension physical rig would. We'd need a hybrid: Vicon/OpenCap-style multi-camera rig PLUS a physically-measured object (e.g., a court line, a paddle of known length, or a checkerboard) in-frame to anchor absolute scale.

findings.claim: MetricHMSR (arXiv 2506.09919, latest revision 2026-06-30) is a recent monocular metric-HMR paper that validates metric depth/scale using two ground-truth sources: (1) a synthetic benchmark 'SynFocal' with precisely known camera focal lengths and rendered ground-truth geometry, and (2) the real-world PROX RGB-D dataset for depth ground truth, reporting AbsRel 0.13 and MAE 0.46 for depth, and PA-MPJPE 33.6mm / MPJPE 53.0mm / PVE 62.7mm on 3DPW.
findings.relevance_to_us: Gives us a concrete, adoptable dual-track validation recipe that is more tractable than a physical calibration rig: (a) a synthetic render round-trip (render a body mesh with known scale/pose/camera through our exact viewer pipeline, feed the synthetic image into SAM-3D-Body, and measure decode error against the known ground truth we authored — this directly tests decode-internal fidelity in isolation from real-world capture noise) and (b) an RGB-D ground truth cross-check if any depth sensor is available. The MPJPE/PVE numbers reported here (33-63mm) also give us an external benchmark ceiling: our 53.5mm p95 mesh-skeleton divergence is roughly in the same range as this paper's reported errors on curated benchmarks, suggesting our number may reflect normal SAM-3D-Body-family decode noise rather than a harness bug uniquely ours — but the 262mm worst-joint outlier is far outside this range and warrants the synthetic round-trip test to isolate it.

dead_ends: Direct searches for 'checkerboard' or 'ArUco marker' ground-truth validation specifically applied to SAM-3D-Body/MHR returned nothing — no such study, blog post, or issue exists; the closest matches were generic ArUco-for-camera-calibration papers unrelated to human mesh scale validation.

No SAM-3D-Body-specific synthetic-render round-trip validation study was found published anywhere (Meta's own paper, per earlier searches, validates against curated annotation-pipeline benchmarks, not a controlled synthetic or physical rig).

Calib3R (arXiv 2509.08813) looked promising by title ('3D Foundation Model for Multi-Camera to Robot Calibration and Metric-Scaled Scene Reconstruction') but is about camera-to-robot calibration for scene reconstruction, not human body mesh decode validation — not fetched further after title screen ruled it out as off-target.

MetricHMSR's 'MetricHMSR' name suggested it might specifically diagnose the SAM-3D-Body decode issue but it is an independent, unrelated monocular metric-HMR model/paper, not an audit of SAM-3D-Body itself; useful only as a methodology template, not as a direct finding about SAM-3D-Body.

maps.angle: Per-stage wall-clock cost decomposition in modern (2026) human-mesh-recovery pipelines: backbone forward pass vs MHR/mesh decode-and-fit vs classical post-chain (tracking/smoothing/world-placement) — used as external analogs since no public source profiles Meta SAM-3D-Body's own stages or a pickleball-specific pipeline.

findings.claim: Fast SAM 3D Body (arXiv 2603.15603, published ~March 2026) profiles the ORIGINAL (unoptimized) 3DB/SAM-3D-Body-family baseline at ~0.8-1.0 FPS (~1000+ ms/frame) on an RTX 6000 Ada, and shows the two largest single-lever latency reductions are (a) replacing the iterative MHR-to-SMPL mesh-fitting/optimization loop with a direct feedforward neural mapping (>10,000x faster for that sub-step, and the paper calls out custom operator-level optimization around this decode path as the single largest cumulative reduction, ~381ms saved) and (b) cutting redundant backbone forward passes from 3 to 1 per person by batching body+hand crops (~371ms saved via switching to a lighter detector/pose front-end). Final optimized pipeline lands at 103-152ms/frame (6.3-9.7 FPS), an 8.2-10.9x speedup.
findings.relevance_to_us: Directly on point: in the SAME model family we run (SAM-3D-Body/MHR), the iterative mesh-decode/fitting step (analogous to our suspect 'latent decode world round-trip') is empirically one of the two dominant wall-time costs in the unoptimized baseline — not a cheap afterthought. This means our suspected decode-fidelity bug sits in a stage that plausibly also eats a large chunk of our 9-10min/clip, so a decode fix (especially if it moves toward direct feedforward projection rather than iterative fitting) could plausibly HELP the 6-8min speed target rather than trade against it, contrary to the gap's implicit worry.

findings.claim: FastHMR (arXiv 2510.10868, Oct 2025) Table 6 per-component ablation on an RTX 3090: backbone+ECLM alone runs at 83.3 FPS, backbone+ECLM+token-merging at 176.5 FPS, but the diffusion decoder/mesh-regression head alone runs at 8211 FPS — i.e., in THAT architecture the decoder is essentially free and the transformer backbone/encoder is overwhelmingly the bottleneck (full pipeline throughput, 103.4 FPS, tracks the backbone number almost exactly).
findings.relevance_to_us: Important counter-datapoint/nuance: whether 'decode' is cheap or expensive is architecture-dependent. FastHMR's feedforward diffusion decoder is nearly free; SAM-3D-Body's (older-style) iterative MHR-to-SMPL fitting is expensive. This tells us our specific 262mm decode bug is likely sitting in the iterative/optimization-style decode path (like stock 3DB), which is the risky, expensive kind — worth flagging when scoping the decode-fidelity fix so the fix owner budgets real profiling time on THIS stage specifically rather than assuming it's negligible like FastHMR's.

findings.claim: OnlineHMR (CVPR 2026, arXiv 2603.17355) frames the standard alternative to its own causal/online design as pipelines that rely on 'future frames or global optimization' for world-grounded alignment, explicitly citing the 'heavy computation and memory cost of long-sequence offline processing' that global/SLAM-style world-grounding optimization imposes, versus its incremental SLAM + causal KV-cache approach built specifically to avoid that heavy optimization cost.
findings.relevance_to_us: Supports the hypothesis that our classical post-chain's world-placement/grounding stage (not just court/stance/smoothing) can be a legitimately heavy, optimization-based cost center in world-grounded HMR pipelines generally — distinct from lightweight per-keypoint smoothing. Useful framing for where in our post-chain to point a profiler first (world-placement/grounding_refine-adjacent logic) rather than assuming stance detection/smoothing dominate.

findings.claim: A generic 2026 video pose-tracking stack survey (RTMDet-tiny detection ~6ms/frame + RTMPose-m pose ~8ms/frame + ByteTrack tracking ~0.3ms/frame + One-Euro/Kalman smoothing ~0.05ms/frame) totals ~14.35ms/frame CPU time at 30fps, meaning classical tracking+smoothing is under 3% of stage time versus detection+pose backbone.
findings.relevance_to_us: Weak analog only — this is a 2D keypoint stack (RTMPose), not a 3D mesh/world-grounding pipeline, so it under-represents cost of our 3D-specific classical stages (court homography, stance/foot-pin, world grounding). Use only to support the narrower claim that pure smoothing/filtering (as opposed to world-placement/registration) is near-free; do not generalize to our full post-chain.

findings.claim: No public source was found that profiles Meta's actual SAM-3D-Body release (as shipped, not the Fast- variant) stage-by-stage, nor any source profiling a pickleball-specific or sports-video 3D pipeline's decode-vs-post-chain-vs-backbone split at the granularity requested.
findings.relevance_to_us: Confirms the original gap is a genuine literature hole for OUR exact stack; the findings above are the best available externally-sourced analogs (same model family baseline timing, and a same-genre architecture's per-component ablation) rather than a direct answer. The team's own harness/profiler run (already flagged as needed) remains the only way to get an exact number for the 9-10min/clip breakdown; these sources bound plausibility (decode-style stages CAN be 30-40%+ of unoptimized latency in this model family) rather than confirming a hard percentage for our clip.

Independent, external confirmation this is a known SAM-3D-*family* decode failure mode, not just our harness.** Sibling repo `facebookresearch/sam-3d-objects` issue #57: a real 5cm cube round-tripped through SAM3D decode came out at ~15cm (3x scale error) **even with accurate depth supplied** — "depth pointmap changed nothing" maps directly onto our own "none-vs-field changed nothing." Compounding: issue #69 ("Children are adults, but very far," opened 2025-11-28, still open) shows the decoder's metric-scale mechanism is a **learned body-proportion-to-depth prior**, not a geometric computation — it has no independent metric anchor. And a third-party audit paper [CORROBORATED, 2 votes] — "Investigating Anthropometric Fidelity in SAM 3D Body" (arXiv:2601.06035, 2025-12-02) — independently finds a "regression to the mean" on individual body shape/scale baked into the MHR low-dim representation + DINOv3 conditioning + annotation-based alignment training. **Net: temper how much further "find the exact scale bug" debugging we sink in vs. accepting some residual as an upstream model-family ceiling** to be worked around via constraints rather than chased to zero.

**F. Actionable validation recipe we don't have today.** MetricHMSR (arXiv:2506.09919, v4 2026-06-30) validates metric decode via a **synthetic benchmark with known ground-truth geometry/focal length** rather than a physical rig. **Recommended: build a synthetic render round-trip test** — author a body mesh with known scale/pose/camera, render it through our viewer pipeline, feed the render back into SAM-3D-Body, measure decode error against the ground truth we authored ourselves. This isolates decode-internal fidelity from real-capture noise and is more tractable than a checkerboard/ArUco physical rig (which, per exhaustive search, nobody has built for this model family — GH issue #47, opened 2025-11-21, is still open and unanswered on exactly this question). Bonus: MetricHMSR's own reported errors (PA-MPJPE 33.6mm / MPJPE 53.0mm / PVE 62.7mm on 3DPW) put our 53.5mm p95 in the *same ballpark as normal SAM-3D-Body-family noise* — but our 262mm worst-joint outlier is far outside this range and is what the synthetic test should specifically hunt.

**G. Fixing decode could help the speed target too, not just trade against it.** Fast SAM 3D Body (arXiv:2603.15603, 2026-03) profiles the *same model family's* unoptimized baseline and finds the iterative MHR-to-SMPL fitting/decode step is one of the two dominant wall-time costs (~381ms of the total ~1000ms/frame baseline) — replacing it with a direct feedforward mapping was >10,000x faster for that sub-step alone. **If our decode fix moves toward a more direct/feedforward path (rather than more iteration), it's plausibly speed-positive** for the 6-8min/clip target, not a tax on it. (Caveat: FastHMR arXiv:2510.10868 shows the opposite is true in a *different* architecture where the decoder is nearly free — decode-cost-share is architecture-dependent, so profile our actual stage before assuming either direction.)

**H. Playback/speed: no better mesh compression path is adoptable today, but the field is quietly moving off meshes entirely.** MPEG V-DMC (FDIS July 2025, IS expected March 2026) is purpose-built for exactly our per-frame human mesh sequence problem (base mesh + per-frame displacement, reusing HEVC/VVC hardware codecs) but **has no browser/JS decoder yet** — calendar flag, not actionable now. More consequential: commercial volumetric-video production (DNE/Gracia, April 2026; Spark 2.0 LoD, 2026) is **moving to 4D Gaussian Splatting instead of meshes specifically because meshes flatten soft-tissue/motion detail** — the same class of fidelity loss we're fighting in our 262mm round-trip. This is a legitimate alternative-rendering-target pivot point to flag if decode-fidelity work stalls further, not an immediate action. Cheaper and immediately actionable if/when we serialize to glTF/GLB: **use MeshOpt + `KHR_mesh_quantization`, not Draco** — Draco doesn't compress morph-target-style per-frame-different-vertex-position animation (our exact case) and decodes slower; MeshOpt is what the field has converged on for animated web meshes.

## 2. WHAT CONFIRMS OUR PLAN

- **VERIFIED=0 stance is externally vindicated.** GH issue #47 (SAM-3D-Body metric-accuracy question) is open and unanswered by Meta as of this pass — no one outside Meta has an answer to "is this metrically accurate," so treating nothing as VERIFIED until we build our own gate is the only defensible position.
- **Latent-space smoothing design direction is validated by independent prior art.** [CORROBORATED, 2 votes] "World-Coordinate Human Motion Retargeting via SAM 3D Body" (arXiv:2512.21573, 2025-12-25) builds the closest published precedent to our exact unwired stage: frozen SAM-3D-Body/MHR backbone, **identity/skeleton-scale params locked across a temporal window**, smoothing done **in MHR latent space** (not decoded mesh/joints — confirmed no public prior art does this either, so we're ahead of the field here, not duplicating it), plus a differentiable soft foot-contact model for the killed grounding stage. This is directionally exactly our unwired-smoothing plan, and per-track identity/scale locking is also a candidate *workaround* for the decode-internal scale defect itself (bypass the bug's symptom rather than chase its cause).
- **Fast-SAM-3D-Body NOT-ADOPT ruling holds up against the newer alternatives found.** SAM-Body4D (arXiv:2512.08406, 2025-12-09, same SAM-3D-Body/MHR base) achieves a modest, safe ~2x batching win via track-then-densify masklet conditioning with *no* reported accuracy regression, and TemPoFast3D (arXiv:2505.07333) gets 3.77x speedup via seed-frame-then-propagate with accuracy held (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS all within tolerance) — both confirm that "speed via smarter frame selection/conditioning" is the safe lever, and "speed via full-model rewrite" (what Fast-SAM-3D-Body did) is the one that broke on our fast-swing case. Our ruling was the right call.
- **grounding_refine being killed and gated on decode is the right sequencing.** OnlineHMR (arXiv:2603.17355, 2026-03) independently frames global/SLAM-style world-grounding optimization as a legitimately heavy, expensive pipeline stage in this problem class generally — not something stance/smoothing dominates. A same-genre physics-refinement paper for fast athletic motion (boxing iLQR, arXiv:2504.08175) shows real promise (foot-float 16.4→8.1mm, foot-slide 2.2→0.3mm with only marginal accuracy cost) but is explicitly downstream of decode and would inherit the 262mm/53.5mm defect — confirms un-kill-after-decode-fix, not before, is correct.
